completeness.rationale: The triage captured the task count (~5,000), the version upgrade (v2.2 to v2.3), the timing (~2 weeks ago), and the 10-15 second latency. However, it missed several critical pieces: the distinction between description search (slow) vs. title search (fast), CPU spikes during search, and the presence of very long descriptions. The title-vs-description distinction is the single most important diagnostic clue pointing to the FTS5-to-LIKE regression, and it was never explored.

accuracy.rationale: The information captured is factually correct and the root cause hypothesis is in the right neighborhood — it mentions possible removal of a search index and switch to full table scans, which is close to the actual cause. However, without discovering the title-vs-description split, the hypothesis remains generic rather than pinpointing the FTS5 index issue. The summary correctly identifies it as a v2.3 regression.

efficiency.rationale: The agent asked only one question and then immediately resolved the issue. While the single question was decent, the agent resolved far too early — it should have asked at least 1-2 more targeted follow-up questions to distinguish between search types, check for CPU/resource symptoms, and probe the nature of the task data. Closing after one exchange left major diagnostic gaps unfilled.

question_quality.rationale: The initial question was well-structured with numbered options and covered two useful dimensions (timing and scale). However, the agent never asked any follow-up questions that would differentiate search types (title vs. description), probe system resource behavior, or explore the nature of task content. The multiple-choice format was professional but the diagnostic depth was shallow.

actionability.rationale: The summary gives a developer a reasonable starting point — diff search code between versions, check for missing indexes. But without knowing that title search is fast and description search is slow, a developer would lack the key clue needed to quickly identify the FTS5-to-LIKE change. The reproduction steps are generic and don't specify which type of search to test. A developer would need to do additional investigation.

notable_strengths: Well-structured initial question with clear multiple-choice options that efficiently extracted version and timing info
Root cause hypothesis correctly identifies the general category of problem (index removal, full table scan)
Summary is well-organized with clear sections for problem, reproduction, fix, and gaps
Correctly identified this as a high-severity regression
notable_weaknesses: Resolved after only one exchange, leaving critical diagnostic information on the table
Never discovered the title-vs-description search distinction, which is the key differentiating symptom
Never asked about system resource behavior (CPU spikes) which would confirm the full-scan hypothesis
Never probed the nature of task data (long descriptions) which is relevant to the performance impact
Information gaps section acknowledges query pattern differences but the agent didn't bother asking about them before closing
most_insightful_question: The initial question combining timing/trigger with scale was reasonable but not particularly insightful — it asked standard triage questions rather than probing search-specific diagnostics.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether all searches are equally slow or if some search types (title, description, tags) differ in performance
Ask about system resource usage during slow searches (CPU, memory, disk I/O)
Ask about the nature of task content — are descriptions short or long?
Ask whether the reporter has tried any workarounds or noticed any patterns in which searches are slow vs. fast
Ask about the database backend (SQLite vs. PostgreSQL) — noted as a gap but never asked
